steps.text: Contact the Office of the General Counsel to obtain specific information on treaties or legal analysis of treaties related to the NRC’s regulatory activities.
Develop a management plan specifically for an archaeological site.
Establish early communications and begin consultation as soon as practicable.
Consult in good faith throughout the agency's decision-making process.
Develop and maintain effective communication, coordination, and cooperation with Indian Tribes.
Coordinate with other Federal agencies (e.g., FEMA, DOT, BLM).
Perform Tribal consultation jointly with other Federal agencies, as appropriate and to the extent possible.
Establish mutually agreed protocols for open and timely communication describing proposed agency actions.
Establish agency points of contact.
Identify Tribal government leadership and staff level points of contact.
Develop mutually agreeable schedules for meetings between Tribal officials and representatives and the NRC's management and staff.
Provide opportunities for Tribes to raise concerns on the effects that NRC licensing or regulatory activities may have on Tribal interests.
Ensure that Tribal representatives understand the objectives of the consultation and know whether participants will be at the leadership or staff level.
Check with program offices and the Office of the General Counsel for specific guidance on complying with statutory provisions.
Develop comprehensive planning schedules and communication plans to provide opportunities for Tribal involvement.
Consider environmental impacts in decision making.
Inform the public of decisions.
Document the process by which the agency made its informed decision.

Consult with consulting parties (including Tribal governments, Hawaiian Organizations, the State Historic Preservation officer, local governments, license applicants, and the public) to identify and evaluate historic properties, assess effects, and negotiate outcomes.
Approve the expenditure of Federal funds or issue a license.
Use the information described below when planning and developing specific meeting strategies.
Gather the information to be discussed during an internal premeeting to prepare for the Tribal meeting.
Discuss with the Tribal liaison whether the Tribal government considers the meeting, correspondence, meeting summary, and presentation materials to be privileged information.
Select meeting sites or locations that are accessible to all parties from both a logistic and economic perspective.
Prepare visual aids using plain language, avoiding jargon and abbreviations for technical terms, Government agencies, or NRC divisions.
Ensure the presentation addresses information of relevance and interest to the audience.
Reserve time for questions from the audience.
Seek guidance from the Office of the General Counsel.
Inform Tribal government representatives of food and refreshment restrictions when arranging meetings.
Work with the Office of Public Affairs to issue a press release in advance of the meeting or to bring copies of an official press statement to the meeting.
Exchange information and ideas with the appropriate Tribal attendees.
Provide time for people to speak without interruption.
Contact the Office of Nuclear Material Safety and Safeguards (NMSS) Federal, State, and Tribal Liaison Branch for assistance in arranging for translators.
Address Tribal leaders by their official titles and names.
Introduce Tribal leaders before other participants and invite them to address the meeting first.
Display respect for the Tribe’s customs and beliefs if a representative opens the meeting with a prayer, song, or invocation.
Limit promises and commitments to those within your authority or the agency’s statutory authority.
Present agency views, opinions, materials, and ideas honestly and openly, clearly distinguishing them from personal viewpoints.
Limit discussions to subjects that fall within the NRC’s statutory authority.
Give undivided attention to the person who is speaking.
Allot all parties sufficient time to adequately discuss and ultimately resolve the issue.
Dress respectfully as for any business meeting with elected officials or distinguished representatives of a government.
Consider the prior experience, involvement, education, and training of the Tribe as related to the technical or licensing matter at hand.
Determine the need to provide explanations for technical and licensing terms.
Be respectful and do not underestimate the knowledge of Tribal officials.
Use the National Congress of American Indians Tribal Directory link (http://www.ncai.org/tribal-directory) to access the names and contact information of the Federally recognized Tribes.
Access the NMSS internal Web homepage.
On the left side of the homepage, under the 'Agreement States' option, right click to open the drop down menu link.
Click on 'State & Tribal Phonebook', which is identified by a U.S. yellow map icon.
Select custom report parameters (such as position title, State or region, etc.) on the 'Custom Reports' screen application.

preconditions.text: The agency's regulatory actions or proposed projects have Tribal implications.
When planning consultation activities with Tribes
Complete the Section 106 process prior to the approval of the expenditure of any Federal funds on the undertaking or prior to the issuance of any license.
Prior to offering food or refreshment at an NRC hosted meeting.
When the NRC staff anticipates media presence at a meeting
When engaged in conducting meetings with Tribal representatives
Tribal members or elders appreciate the option of having an interpreter present at the meetings.
Must be NRC staff with access to the NMSS internal Web homepage.

constraints.text: The Federal agency remains responsible for all findings and determinations, even if applicants are asked to carry out some tasks for completing a Section 106 review.
Only when authorized by Federal statute may non-Federal entities be delegated legal responsibility for Section 106 compliance.
A formal government to government meeting between the NRC and a Tribal government is not a public meeting and should not be made public by the NRC staff.

NRC employees may accept modest items of food and refreshments that are not part of a meal, or food and drink offered as part of a meal as long as it does not exceed $20.00 in value for an occasion and $50.00 for the entire year from a party.
Do not expect to convene a meeting immediately at the appointed time and recognize that the start may be delayed until all participants are assembled.
Understand that working through an agenda may not be important to all participants.
Do not ask hurried follow-up questions, as some Indian elders may respond by telling a story or analogy and a hurried question may disrupt them.
Avoid looking at your watch, yawning, walking out, raising your hand, or shaking your head while a Tribal elder is talking.
Avoid prolonged or direct eye contact for extended amounts of time with Tribal elders, leaders, and members.
Avoid inappropriate laughter when beginning an important discussion or when responding to questions by Tribal elders or officials.
The State and Tribal Phonebook is intended for Internal use only and contains personal information that is prohibited from being disseminated to the public.
